title: Dog Swollen Hock Joint: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's hock warm and swollen? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why taking a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A warm, swollen hock indicates inflammation or infection and typically requires a veterinary visit within 24–48 hours; however, it is an emergency if your dog is non-weight bearing or lethargic.

What does it mean if my dog has a swollen hock joint?

The hock is essentially your dog's ankle, located on the hind leg between the knee and the paw. When this joint becomes warm to the touch and visibly swollen, it indicates an inflammatory response. This can be caused by various issues, including soft tissue injuries like sprains, joint fluid buildup (effusion), arthritis, or more serious conditions such as septic arthritis (an infection in the joint) or immune-mediated diseases.

Is a warm, swollen hock joint in my dog a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for a warm, swollen hock is Medium, typically requiring a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. While it may not be a life-threatening emergency, inflammation accompanied by heat often suggests an active infection or significant trauma. You should seek immediate emergency care if your dog exhibits:

  • Complete inability to put weight on the leg
  • Lethargy or depressed behavior
  • An open wound near the swelling

How can taking a photo of my dog's swollen leg help the veterinarian?

Taking a clear photo or video of the affected leg can be a vital tool for your veterinarian. A visual record assists the triage process in several ways:

  • It helps the clinical team assess the degree of swelling by comparing it to the opposite, healthy leg.
  • It allows the vet to track the progression of the inflammation from the moment it was first noticed.
  • It helps the medical team determine if the condition is localized to the joint or spreading through the rest of the limb.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Swollen hock joints in dogs can be associated with polyarthritis, characterized by fever, lameness, lethargy, and inappetence. Diagnosis of polyarthritis involves radiography (joint effusion, possible erosive bone destruction) and abnormal joint fluid analyses (increased cell counts). Joint trauma, such as cranial cruciate ligament rupture, can also cause hock swelling, potentially alongside plasmacytic-lymphocytic synovitis. Osteochondrosis dessicans (OCD) of the tarsocrural joint may present with variable amounts of joint effusion and lameness. Serous tarsitis, characterized by soft, fluctuating swellings between the ligaments of the femorotarsal joint, is another potential cause, although it typically does not cause pain or lameness.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human aspirin for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your dog human pain relief medications without specific instructions from a veterinarian, as many are toxic to dogs and can cause stomach ulcers or kidney failure.

faqs.question: How can I keep my dog comfortable until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: Restrict your dog's activity to prevent further injury. Avoid long walks or jumping, and provide a soft, padded area for them to rest.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose the swollen hock?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely perform a physical exam and may recommend X-rays or a joint tap (fine needle aspirate) to analyze the fluid inside the joint for signs of infection or inflammation.
